title: Cyberlibel and Maria Ressa
primary_topics: Austin vs Pound schools of jurisprudence
sub_topics: presumption of malice for private citizens
Act No. 3326 (12-year prescription for special laws)
John Austin's 'dura lex sed lex' positivism

stances.claim: Judge Montesa's 37-page, 170-footnoted decision is not baseless or capricious — it correctly applied 'law times facts equals decision' formula and adhered to John Austin's positivist legal philosophy.

The article was indeed libelous because Keng is a private citizen, so malice is presumed by law — the burden of proof shifted to the accused who 'miserably failed' to rebut the presumption, and oddly chose not to testify to show 'fair comment on a matter of public interest.'
stances.rhetorical_move: doctrinal-burden analysis + critique of defense strategy

stances.claim: The conviction nonetheless deserves appeal because the Supreme Court can acquit based on prescription if it sidesteps John Austin's strict syllogism and embraces Roscoe Pound's sociological school — which regards law as 'a brick — an important one — in the building of a just and humane society.'
stances.rhetorical_move: school-of-jurisprudence appeal (Austin vs Pound) + brick-in-edifice metaphor (echoing Salonga teaching)

signature_phrases: Roscoe Pound's more liberal sociological school
law merely as a brick — an important one — in the building of a just and humane society
my esteemed guru, a respected scholar, low-profile educator, brilliant lawyer, outstanding lawmaker, acclaimed patriot, and statesman par excellence
register_markers: ALL CAPS phrase for emphasis (MALICE IS PRESUMED BY THE LAW)
decision_framework_signals: applies Austin-vs-Pound jurisprudential opposition as the meta-question framing legal-policy choices
frames legal-positivist syllogism ('law times facts') as inadequate when sociological consequences matter
uses 'law as brick in social edifice' metaphor (transmitting Salonga's teaching) as the more humane alternative
applies private-citizen-malice-presumption doctrine as default analytic rule
